data.title: Invesco expects $3.275B 2026 operating expenses as it targets payout ratio near 60%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Invesco Ltd. (IVZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Andrew Schlossberg (President, CEO & Director) said Invesco’s strategic focus remained on “profitable organic growth,” emphasizing “high demand, scalable investment capabilities like fixed income, and delivery vehicles like ETFs,” alongside “Asia Pacific presence” and “a strong performing and growing EMEA business,” and added that private markets offerings were being “augmented by our recently announced partnerships with Barings and LGT Capital.”
  • Schlossberg highlighted flow momentum in volatile markets, reporting “net long-term inflows were $21.8 billion,” with “$11.6 billion in global liquidity inflows,” and said Invesco delivered “the 11th straight quarter of net inflows.”
  • Schlossberg described ETF scale and active ETF expansion, stating “ending AUM stood at a record $638 billion,” with “nearly $19 billion of net inflows,” and added, “we launched 4 new active ETFs this quarter,” while noting the “active ETF platform currently managing over $20 billion in assets.”
  • On QQQ competition, Schlossberg said NASDAQ allowing two additional ETFs to track the NASDAQ 100 was “an evolution of a highly successful benchmark,” adding, “our installed base is tough to erode,” and emphasizing “any competitor fund will pay the same amount” for index licensing while “existing licensing agreements are not impacted.”
  • Schlossberg cited China JV and private markets milestones, saying the China JV reached “a record high AUM of $142 billion” with “$8.7 billion of net long-term inflows,” while private markets posted “$400 million of net inflows,” and INCREF “with leverage, now total $5 billion.”
  • Allison Dukes (Senior MD & CFO) reported improved profitability and capital return actions, saying “operating margin improving to 34.5%,” “adjusted diluted earnings per share was $0.57,” and that Invesco “increased the amount of common share repurchases… buying back $40 million,” while adding, “our Board authorized an additional $1 billion in common share repurchases.”

Outlook

  • Dukes guided, “we expect operating expenses for 2026 to be in the $3.275 billion range, under flat markets from the higher April AUM level… in the $2.3 trillion range,” and added, “we still believe that our operating expense base is approximately 25% variable in relation to changes in net revenue.”
  • On taxes, Dukes said, “for the second quarter, we estimate our non-GAAP effective tax rate will be in the 25% to 26% range, excluding any discrete items.”
  • On the hybrid investment platform, Dukes stated, “we expect one-time implementation quarterly costs to continue in the $10 million to $15 million range per quarter,” and added that incremental expense “will build towards $10 million per quarter later this year,” with “expected completion by the end of 2026.”
  • Versus the prior quarter’s messaging around hybrid platform savings, Dukes’ Q1 framing added specificity on timing and magnitude, saying, “we expect the cost saved to be at least $60 million in calendar year 2027.”

Financial Results

  • Dukes said quarter-end AUM was “$2.2 trillion,” and that “volatility drove a $42 billion decline in AUM for the quarter,” largely offset by “continued strong net long-term asset inflows of $22 billion,” while adding that April market recovery brought AUM “into the $2.3 trillion range more recently.”
  • Dukes reported “net revenue of $1.3 billion,” and attributed the year-over-year increase primarily to “investment management fees… driven by higher average AUM and the reclassification of QQQ to fee earnings.”
  • On profitability drivers, Dukes said, “this drove 500 basis points of positive operating leverage,” and cited expense items including “a $33 million increase in compensation expense” tied to “incremental changes to our retirement eligibility criteria,” and “marketing expenses… $21 million higher due to the marketing associated with the QQQ.”
  • On yield and mix, Dukes provided, “the net revenue yield was 22.9 basis points… and the exit yield… was 22.8 basis points,” adding the firm was “approaching a degree of stabilization or an inflection point.”
  • On balance sheet actions, Dukes stated, “we redeemed a $500 million senior note,” and noted quarter-end “$1.1 billion drawn on the revolving credit facility,” adding, “we expect to reduce the amount drawn on the revolver as the year progresses.”

Q&A

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: asked about QQQ securities lending; President Schlossberg responded, “we don’t see that as a huge opportunity.”
  • Hawken, BMO: asked about multi-year strategic priorities; President Schlossberg pointed to “personalization,” “income,” non-U.S. “money in motion,” and “private markets into… defined contribution,” adding Invesco would “lean into even more in 2026.”
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: pressed for more expense detail; CFO Dukes reiterated 2026 expenses “based on that AUM level… $2.3 trillion,” flagged Canada closing expectations, and said the CI transition implies “a loss of operating income… $5 million to $10 million” per quarter in the back half of 2026.
  • Fannon, Jefferies: asked about rising platform fees; President Schlossberg said, “we don’t see this having a material impact at all,” and added platform fees considered would be “focused on new assets.”
  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore: asked about durability of non-U.S. growth; President Schlossberg cited decades-long presence and said reforms in the U.K. were driving “meaningful mandates… solution-oriented.”
  • Schorr, Evercore: asked about international QQQ extensions; President Schlossberg said, “we extended the Q lineup last year in Hong Kong, and this year, it’s going to be in Japan,” and CFO Dukes added, “the marketing behind that… has been significant.”
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked about QQQ fee competition; President Schlossberg emphasized “total cost of ownership” including “tightness of the spreads” and brand, and said, “we’ll make sure we remain competitive.”
  • Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked China product pipeline and fee dynamics; President Schlossberg said, “we launched 14 new products this quarter,” while CFO Dukes said China fee rates could be “slightly lower” over time but “the margins continue to improve there.”
  • Brian Bedell, Deutsche Bank: asked about QQQ institutional vs retail pricing and marketing; President Schlossberg said different price points were “not possible in the ETF space per se,” but “separate accounts… could be at different price points,” while CFO Dukes reaffirmed marketing expectations “in the range of $60 million to $100 million.”
  • William Katz, TD Cowen: asked about longer-term margin potential; CFO Dukes said, “we needed to get the margin back to the mid-30s on a path to high 30s,” adding “we’ve got our sights focused on how do we get back to the high 30s.”
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: asked about further portfolio pruning; CFO Dukes said, “we don’t know that there’s a lot more pruning to be done,” while reiterating Canada’s near-term operating income impact.
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: asked about AI; President Schlossberg said AI was used for “data analysis… content creation… operational efficiency,” adding, “we estimate that close to 80% of our employees… are using these tools every day.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated drilling into expense visibility and QQQ competitive economics, including requests for “a little bit further in terms of detail” (Daniel Fannon, Jefferies) and scenarios around “lower price point” entrants (Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident in prepared remarks and largely steady in Q&A, using categorical language on competitive impact (“we don’t see this having a material impact at all,” President Schlossberg) and profitability trajectory (“we feel good about the momentum,” CFO Dukes).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management discussion shifted from recounting 2025 transformations to defending/clarifying QQQ competitive dynamics and providing more granular operating expense and platform-cost framing, while analysts continued to focus heavily on expenses and profitability path.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 emphasized defending QQQ’s moat amid new NASDAQ 100 licensing, with President Schlossberg saying, “our installed base is tough to erode,” a topic not present in the Q4 prepared narrative, which focused on completing the QQQ conversion.
  • Expense guidance became more explicit in Q1, with CFO Dukes providing a single 2026 operating expense figure (“$3.275 billion range”), while Q4 framed $3.2 billion as a “base to start with for 2026” and emphasized moving parts.
  • Flow commentary stayed constructive quarter-to-quarter, with Q1 reporting “net long-term inflows… $21.8 billion” versus Q4 reporting “$19 billion” of net long-term inflows, while Q1 added more discussion of QQQ flow normalization and April re-acceleration.
  • Analysts maintained recurring themes quarter-to-quarter—expenses, margin trajectory, platform economics, and China JV durability—while Q1 added incremental scrutiny on QQQ competition and international expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited market and geopolitical uncertainty, with President Schlossberg describing “heightened volatility as geopolitical uncertainty, sharp moves in energy prices and changing interest rate expectations weighed on public markets.”
  • On QQQ competitive risk, management’s mitigation centered on liquidity/brand and switching frictions, with President Schlossberg stressing “unmatched liquidity” and that “switching costs are higher than assumed with taxes being a major factor.”
  • On Canada transition risk, CFO Dukes flagged a near-term earnings headwind, describing a “modestly negative operating income impact” of “$5 million to $10 million” per quarter initially, with improvement expected “over time” via sub-advisory growth.
  • On private credit headlines, President Schlossberg said “the headlines are oftentimes drowning out the fundamentals,” and added Invesco’s alternative credit platform had “0 software exposure,” while emphasizing “we have dry powder, diversification and extensive experience.”

Final Takeaway

Invesco’s management highlighted continued organic inflows and scaling in ETFs, China JV, and select private markets products, while sharpening investor focus on a defined 2026 expense outlook of $3.275 billion under flat markets and an ongoing capital return framework targeting a total payout ratio near 60%. Leadership spent substantial time addressing QQQ competitive developments—arguing liquidity, brand, and investor switching frictions support durability—while also outlining a concrete runway for hybrid platform implementation costs through 2026 and at least $60 million of cost savings in calendar 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: W. P. Carey stock gains on beat-and-raise Q1
data.description:
W. P. Carey (WPC) was trading higher after a beat-and-raise first quarter, primarily attributed to strong net investment activity, rent escalations, and lease-related income.

Shares were 0.06% higher at $72.70 during after-market trading on Tuesday.

Ranked as one of the largest net lease REITs, WPC reported adjusted FFO per share of $1.30, up 11.1% from $1.17 in the first quarter of 2025 and above the S&P Cap IQ consensus estimate of $1.27.

The rise in earnings was primarily attributed to the accretive impact of net investment activity, rent escalations, and higher other lease-related income.

Revenue increased to $454.51M from $409.86M a year ago, exceeding the consensus of $447.18M.

Lease revenues increased to $402.83M from $353.77M a year ago due to net investment activity and rent escalations. Income from finance leases and loans receivable was also up to $27.69M from $17.46M on net investment activity.

However, operating property revenue decreased to $12.05M from $33.09M on account of the sale of the company's self-storage operating portfolio.

Investment management revenue fell to $1.49M from $2.42M.

Impairment charges on the real estate segment were up to $40.01M during the quarter, compared to $6.85M a year ago.

As of March 31, the company's net lease portfolio had an occupancy rate of 98.1%.

The New York-based company raised its full-year 2026 guidance range for adjusted FFO per share to $5.16-$5.26, vs. $5.20 consensus. The previous guidance range stood at $5.13 to $5.23.

The guidance considers higher expected investment volume and lower estimated potential rent loss from tenant credit events.

Earnings call is scheduled for Wednesday, April 29, 11:00 AM ET.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Bloom Energy Q1 smashes earnings and revenue estimates, lifts FY26 guidance
TWO Harbors agrees to CrossCountry's increased offer of $11.30/share
data.description:
Two Harbors Investment (TWO) agreed to an amended merger agreement with CrossCountry Mortgage, increasing the all-cash deal to $11.30 a share from $10.80. 

The amended deal comes after TWO Board’s "thorough evaluation" of an unsolicited competing proposal received on April 20 from mortgage wholesale UWM Holdings (UWMC), according to a  statement on Tuesday.

“Our increased bid reflects our continued excitement for this transaction and our strong conviction in the strategic and financial merits of combining CCM and TWO Harbors,” said Ron Leonhardt, Founder and CEO of CrossCountry Mortgage

The special meeting of shareholders for the transaction is set for May 19. The transaction is expected to close in the third quarter.

Houlihan Lokey is acting as financial advisor and Jones Day is acting as legal counsel to TWO. Citi is acting as exclusive financial advisor and Simpson Thacher & Bartlett is acting as legal counsel to CCM.

data.title: Purple Innovation updates 2026 revenue outlook to $465M-$485M while maintaining $20M-$30M adjusted EBITDA guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Purple Innovation (PRPL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We entered 2026 building on the progress we made in the fourth quarter, and our first quarter reflects continued progress and greater consistency across our channels" (CEO Robert DeMartini), adding, "We're entering the second quarter with improving trends and are positioned for a step-up in performance."
  • "In the first quarter, total sales were down 8%" (CEO DeMartini), and he said, "E-commerce trends improved sequentially, declining 10% in the first quarter compared with down 15% in the prior period," while noting, "Showroom performance remained a bright spot with sales up 5% and comps up 7%, marking our third consecutive quarter of positive comp growth."
  • "Turning to our outlook. We're updating our revenue guidance to a range of $465 million to $485 million from the prior range of $500 million to $520 million due to the accounting-related adjustment" (CEO DeMartini), while stating, "We're maintaining our adjusted EBITDA guidance of $20 million to $30 million."
  • "Net revenue for the first quarter was $95.7 million, down 8.1% year-over-year" (CFO Todd Vogensen), and he added, "Excluding this accounting-related impact, net revenue would have been $100.6 million or down 3.4% year-over-year."
  • "Todd... will be stepping down as CFO effective May 1" (CEO DeMartini), and he said, "We're also pleased to welcome Bob Lucian as our next CFO."

Outlook

  • "We're updating our full year revenue guidance to a range of $465 million to $485 million from the prior range of $500 million to $520 million due to the accounting-related adjustment discussed earlier" (CFO Vogensen), while reiterating, "We are maintaining our adjusted EBITDA guidance in the range of $20 million to $30 million."
  • "The impact to Q1 was only $5 million" (CFO Vogensen), and he said, "You can see that we adjusted the revenue guidance by about $35 million."
  • Compared with Q4 2025, "For the full year, we expect revenue in the range of $500 million to $520 million and adjusted EBITDA of $20 million to $30 million" (CEO Robert DeMartini, Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 guidance language changed to: "We're updating our revenue guidance" to $465 million to $485 million "due to the accounting-related adjustment" (CEO DeMartini, Q1 2026).

Financial Results

  • "The accounting-related reclassification had no impact on gross profit dollars, EBITDA or cash flow, but it reduced net revenues and cost of sales by the same amount" (CFO Vogensen).
  • "Gross margin for the quarter was approximately 36.8%" (CFO Vogensen), and he attributed the rate to Royale floor models and manufacturing overhead, adding, "As production and shipments normalize, we expect gross margin to return to approximately 40% by the second half of the year."
  • "Operating expenses in the quarter were $52 million, down 6.3% versus $55.5 million last year" (CFO Vogensen), while noting the quarter included "higher spend related to the ongoing evaluation of strategic alternatives."
  • "Our first quarter adjusted loss per share was $0.13 compared to an adjusted loss per share of $0.11 last year" (CFO Vogensen), and "Adjusted EBITDA in the first quarter was negative $4.8 million" (CFO Vogensen).
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $25 million versus $24.3 million on December 31, 2025" (CFO Vogensen), and he called it "the best first quarter cash performance in 7 years" while adding, "Net inventories on March 31, 2026, were $58.1 million, down 2.7% compared to December 31, 2025."

Q&A

  • Taylor Zick (for Brad Thomas), KeyBanc: Can you discuss Q1 demand trends and why you’re confident in a Q2 step-up? CEO DeMartini: "January was fairly healthy. February got a little bit of choppy and then March got a little bit better across all channels," adding, "the consumer still is pretty nervous right now... the category is not robust."
  • Taylor Zick (for Brad Thomas), KeyBanc: What are you seeing in input costs and transportation pressures? CFO Vogensen: "we are seeing pressure across transportation as well as some of our input costs, including foam," but said those headwinds are "being roughly offset with savings that we're seeing on tariffs" and that the company should manage "within our guidance" if oil stays "around that $100 a barrel range."
  • Daniel Silverstein, UBS: Is the sales guidance change underlying demand or just reporting, and how is wholesale trending excluding new doors? CFO Vogensen: "it is purely just the reporting change" and "no change to the underlying activity"; CEO DeMartini said "our top 8 accounts, including Costco, Mattress Firm... are performing up year-on-year" but added, "we've got some smaller accounts that we got to figure out how to service better."
  • Matt Koranda, ROTH: What are you doing to sustain e-commerce improvement after March was flat? CEO DeMartini: "I don't know if it's too early... to call 1 month of trend," adding, "We've enrolled an outside agency... the early signals are good, but I'm not going to wave any success flag yet."
  • Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer: Will the accounting change continue in future quarters, and what sized the Q1 gross margin headwinds? CFO Vogensen: "it will be ongoing" and "if anything, the adjustment gets bigger"; on margin, "The impact from the floor models was about 200 basis points" and lower absorption was "close to 200 basis points of drag."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on accounting optics and sustainability, including "is there going to be a similar type impact in subsequent quarters?" (Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer) and "is anything changing from an underlying demand perspective or it's just a reporting adjustment?" (Daniel Silverstein, UBS).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A, including "we're positioned for a step-up in performance" (CEO Robert DeMartini) alongside "I don't know if it's too early... to call 1 month of trend" (CEO DeMartini) and "the consumer still is pretty nervous right now" (CEO DeMartini).
  • Versus Q4 2025, management’s framing remained execution-led ("not by a recovery in the broader market" (CEO Robert DeMartini, Q4 2025)), but Q1 2026 introduced more sensitivity to reporting and input costs, including "purely just the reporting change" (CFO Todd Vogensen, Q1 2026) and "we are seeing pressure" in foam and transportation (CFO Vogensen).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted primarily in reported revenue: "we expect revenue in the range of $500 million to $520 million" (CEO Robert DeMartini, Q4 2025) changed to "updating our revenue guidance to a range of $465 million to $485 million" (CEO DeMartini, Q1 2026), while adjusted EBITDA guidance stayed "$20 million to $30 million" in both quarters (CEO DeMartini).
  • Gross margin commentary became more transitional in Q1: Q4 described "gross margin... approximately 41.9%" (CFO Todd Vogensen, Q4 2025), while Q1 reported "approximately 36.8%" and tied it to "Royale floor models" and overhead absorption, with an expectation to return "to approximately 40% by the second half of the year" (CFO Vogensen, Q1 2026).
  • Management changes emerged in Q1 with "stepping down as CFO effective May 1" and naming "Bob Lucian as our next CFO" (CEO Robert DeMartini), which was not present in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • "we are seeing pressure across transportation as well as some of our input costs, including foam" (CFO Todd Vogensen), and he said the company is mitigating with tariff savings and sourcing work, adding, "we should be able to manage within our guidance."
  • "Gross margins came in below our normal 40% baseline" due to "higher levels of floor model discounts" tied to the Purple Royale rollout (CEO Robert DeMartini), which management characterized as "temporary" and expected to normalize.
  • "the consumer still is pretty nervous right now" (CEO DeMartini), and he said, "the category is not robust," framing demand as still uncertain.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as continued operational progress with channel mix shifting toward showrooms and premium products, while reported revenue and margin were pressured by an ongoing accounting-related wholesale reclassification and temporary Purple Royale floor-model dynamics. The company lowered 2026 reported revenue guidance to $465 million to $485 million for the accounting change while maintaining adjusted EBITDA guidance of $20 million to $30 million, and it outlined mitigation actions on input costs alongside a CFO transition from Todd Vogensen to Bob Lucian.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
